coredata.dc:title: Real-time detection of alcohol vapors and volatile organic compounds via optical electronic nose using carbon dots prepared from rice husk and density functional theory calculation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Thongsai, Nichaphat
Tanawannapong, Nattapong
Praneerad, Janjira
Kladsomboon, Sumana
Jaiyong, Panichakorn
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: Rice husk, an agricultural waste that currently finds few uses, is rich in cellulose-based materials and silica. In this work, a simple one-pot method for preparing carbon dots and mesoporous silica from rice husk was developed, using hydrothermal and calcination methods. The carbon dots exhibited blue emission with excellent photostability, and had a diameter of 4–5 nm and a quantum yield of 3%. They were demonstrated to be capable of detecting alcohol vapors at room temperature, and of distinguishing between methanol, ethanol, and several volatile organic compounds when used as the sensing layer in an optical electronic nose system. The alcohol content of a commercial beverage was successfully determined using the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose. The solvation effect of the alcohol vapors on the electronic absorption spectra of model carbon dot structures was illustrated using time-dependent density functional theory with the dielectric polarizable continuum model. The UV–vis and computational results confirmed that the sensing mechanism of carbon dots is through the modulation of their optical absorbance governed by polar-polar interfacial interactions. This was experimental and computational demonstration of carbon dot sensing of vapors. Their excellent biocompatibility suggests biomedical applications, in addition to sensing. The production of two functional materials from a single low-value waste source was demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rice husk
Mesoporous silica
Carbon dot
Alcohol sensor
Time-dependent density functional theory

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and mesoporous silica
Optical electronic nose setup and measurement
Synthesis of carbon dots and mesoporous silica

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of optical electronic nose system used in this work.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of mesoporous silica and carbon dots from rice husk.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XRD pattern of mesoporous silica, (c) size distribution of mesoporous silica from DLS, and (d) SEM image of mesoporous silica.

(a) FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots, (b) survey XPS spectrum of carbon dots, (c) the high resolution of C1s peaks, and (d) TEM (left) and HR-TEM (right) images of carbon dots. The inset of (d) displays size distribution of carbon dots from TEM image.

(a) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelengths from 310 to 460 nm. The inset image in (a) shows blue emission of carbon dot solution under UV light (365 nm), (b) relative fluorescence intensity of 0.05 g L−1 carbon dot solution on visible light for four weeks, (c) relative absorbance and fluorescence intensity of 0.05 g L−1 carbon dot solution on UV irradiation (365 nm) in different times, and (d) % cell viability of MCF-7 cells after the incubation with carbon dots in various concentration for 72 h measured by MTT assay.

(a) Gas sensing responses of carbon dot film under blue-green light (472 nm), (b) PCA two-dimensional score plots for ethanol (EtOH), methanol (MeOH), and their mixtures at different ratios, (c) UV–vis absorbance of carbon dot thin film in the presence of methanol and ethanol vapor. Black solid line in (c) represents absorption spectrum of carbon aqueous solution.

Gas sensing responses of carbon dot film under white light for (a) different concentrations of ethanol and spirit sample (35% ethanol), (b) different concentrations of methanol, (c) PCA two-dimensional score plots of standard ethanol solutions at different concentrations and spirit sample (35% alcohol), and (d) standard methanol solution at different concentrations.

(a) Gas sensing responses of carbon dot film under blue-green light (472 nm), after exposure to solvent vapors, (b) PCA two-dimensional score plots, and (c) UV–vis spectra of carbon dot solutions dissolved in various solvents.

Model structures and corresponding calculated absorption spectra in gas phase and implicit solvents of (a), (c) pristine graphene nanoflake and (b), (d) nitrogen-doped graphene nanoflake with edge functionalization.
Pictorial representation of modulation of optical absorption of carbon dots when exposed to solvent molecules.

head.ce:title: Real-time detection of alcohol vapors and volatile organic compounds via optical electronic nose using carbon dots prepared from rice husk and density functional theory calculation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiological Technology, Faculty of Medical Technology, Mahidol University, Bangkok, 73170, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rice husk, an agricultural waste that currently finds few uses, is rich in cellulose-based materials and silica. In this work, a simple one-pot method for preparing carbon dots and mesoporous silica from rice husk was developed, using hydrothermal and calcination methods. The carbon dots exhibited blue emission with excellent photostability, and had a diameter of 4–5 nm and a quantum yield of 3%. They were demonstrated to be capable of detecting alcohol vapors at room temperature, and of distinguishing between methanol, ethanol, and several volatile organic compounds when used as the sensing layer in an optical electronic nose system. The alcohol content of a commercial beverage was successfully determined using the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose. The solvation effect of the alcohol vapors on the electronic absorption spectra of model carbon dot structures was illustrated using time-dependent density functional theory with the dielectric polarizable continuum model. The UV–vis and computational results confirmed that the sensing mechanism of carbon dots is through the modulation of their optical absorbance governed by polar-polar interfacial interactions. This was experimental and computational demonstration of carbon dot sensing of vapors. Their excellent biocompatibility suggests biomedical applications, in addition to sensing. The production of two functional materials from a single low-value waste source was demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Rice is a major source of carbohydrates and is consumed worldwide. Globally, almost 120 million tons of husk is produced each year [1]. This renewable biomass has been used as a fertilizer, as an additive in cement, in concrete production, as a source of activated carbon, and as an insulating material [2–4]. However, the bulk of the husk is discarded as an agricultural waste or burnt, producing greenhouse gases such as methane, carbon monoxide, and carbon dioxide. Techniques are therefore needed to add value to this byproduct, allowing it to find practical applications. There is an increasing interest in the use of agricultural wastes for the preparation of high-value functional materials. As rice husk is rich in silica, the preparation of low-cost silica in industrial quantities is of particular interest. Silica is a polymer consisting of SiO4 units with the general formula SiO2. When prepared from rice husk, it provides a highly porous material with a large surface area, high stability, and low toxicity, and is compatible with many other compounds [2]. Silica has been used in catalysis, drug delivery, chemical separation, insulation, food preparation, and sensing. It has been also used in the construction industry, as an adsorbent of heavy metals and organic pollutants, as a reinforcement and additive in composite materials, and as a material in biomedical devices [5]. Rice husk is also rich in carbon-based materials such as cellulose, hemicelluloses, and lignin, which serve to reinforce the cell walls. These carbon-based materials can be used to synthesize the optically unique carbon-based nanomaterials known as carbon dots.

Since their discovery in 2004, carbon dots have been widely studied and have found numerous applications as bio-imaging probes, drug and gene delivery systems, bio- and chemo-sensors, fluorescent inks, and in optoelectronic devices, energy storage, and photocatalysis [6–11]. As fluorescent materials, carbon dots are considered superior to organic fluorophores and metal-based quantum dots because they offer stable optical properties, high aqueous solubility, chemical stability, tunable excitation and emission, low cytotoxicity, simple preparation and functionalization, and antioxidant properties [12]. The thermal processes used in their production have included pyrolysis/combustion, solvothermal, hydrothermal, and microwave techniques [7,13,14]. Recent trends in carbon dot systems revolve around the use of relatively cheap and abundant precursors. Raw materials, including natural precursors [15], chemical precursors [16], and wastes [17] have been employed. If carbon dots can be prepared from readily-available raw materials, scaling-up and mass production can be achieved, making rice husk an excellent choice as precursor.

Few studies have investigated the synthesis of carbon dots from rice husk. Pandey et al. reported the synthesis of mesoporous silica oxide/carbon dot complexes using pyrolysis [18]. The complexes were biocompatible and could be used in bioimaging. More recently, Ngu et al. reported the synthesis of carbon dots from rice husk and their use in Sn2+ sensor [19]. Wang et al. described the preparation of carbon dot-grafted silica nanoparticles from rice husk [20]. However, effective extraction of carbon dots and silica has not yet been demonstrated. In this work, we describe a method for simultaneously preparing mesoporous silica and carbon dots from rice husk in a one-pot fashion. Due to their photostability, highly interactive polarity, and high surface area, carbon dots are sensitive probes with unique reversible binding properties for use in sensing applications. Although carbon dots are widely regarded as promising fluorophores, there has been little use of their optical absorptivity for sensing, and information on the surface and interfacial interactions that affect their optical properties is scarce. The optical absorptivity of carbon dots offers several advantages. There is no need to maximize their fluorescent quantum yields by carrying out tedious synthesis procedures because their fluorescent properties are surface sensitive, leading to batch-to-batch variation. The novelty of this work is that carbon dots were derived from rice husk and used for vapor sensing in an optical electronic nose, by monitoring changes in optical absorptivity. Methanol and ethanol are the two simplest alcohols and are used as fuels, as precursors for the synthesis of various chemicals, and ethanol in beverages. Prolonged exposure to and excessive accumulation of alcohol vapors can cause irritation, intoxication, and death. The electronic nose is a fast and convenient tool that is able to reproduce the human olfactory system and is widely employed in gas and vapor sensing [21–23]. Due to their stable optical and hydrophilic properties, carbon dots in this work were used as sensing probes in optical electronic nose systems, leveraging the change in optical absorbance upon exposure to alcohol vapor. However, their use as a vapor sensor is rare. Dolai et al. reported an aerogel sensor prepared from carbon dots for the detection of aromatic volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [24]. We recently reported the use of enokitake mushroom- and yogurt-derived carbon dots as vapor probes [25,26], and the development of a carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose as a versatile, real-time device for vapor detection at room temperature. In addition to alcohol vapor sensing, the carbon dots developed in this work were able to distinguish between the VOCs: acetone, diethyl ether, toluene, dicholoromethane, and hexane, and between different ratios between methanol and ethanol. Their sensing was further confirmed by UV–vis spectroscopy and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. For the first time, the sensitivity of carbon dots to vapors was demonstrated both experimentally and computationally. The importance of interfacial electronic coupling between the carbon dots and alcohol vapors was also investigated. Overall, we demonstrated that carbon dots from rice husk could be integrated into an optical electronic nose and used for the detection of alcohol vapors and VOCs. This killing-two-birds-with-one-stone strategy can be scaled up and extended to other silica and carbon rich materials, including sugarcane bagasse and wheat straw. The abundance of these natural precursors makes mass production of mesoporous silica and carbon dots a practical proposition.

Rice husk was obtained from a local rice mill in Lop Buri, Thailand, and Thai spirit (35% v/v alcohol content) from a local supermarket. Nitric acid (65%) was obtained from Carlo Erba. Other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. Cellulose dialysis membrane (1000 Da molecular weight cut-off) was purchased from Spectrum Labs. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

The crushed rice husk was demineralized by overnight immersion first in a 10% HCl solution then in a 30% H2SO4 solution. It was washed with plenty of water to remove contaminants and acid residues until the husk became neutral, then dried in an oven at 50 °C for 48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and mesoporous silica
ce:section.ce:para: The dried husk (10 g) was mixed with 2.5 M nitric acid (50 mL) and sonicated for 15 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The dark brown mixture was then cooled to room temperature, neutralized using a 10 M NaOH solution, and filtered. The residue was preserved for the synthesis of mesoporous silica. The filtrate was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, to remove large particles. The solution was purified using the dialysis membrane, then freeze dried to yield carbon dots in the form of a brown solid (0.93 g, 9.3%).

The residue was washed with DI water, then refluxed with 30% sulfuric acid for 2 h, before washing with DI water until the pH became neutral. It was then pyrolyzed at 700 °C for 4 h to yield mesoporous silica in the form of a white solid (3.74 g, 37.4%).

Cytotoxicity testing of the carbon dots was carried out by MTT assay. MCF-7 breast cancer cells were cultured in 96 well plates at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well. The cell lines were treated for 72 h in media with four different concentrations of carbon dots: 10, 20, 40, and 80 μg mL−1. The cells were then washed with 1X PBS and incubated in 100 μL of 0.5 g L−1 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) at 37 °C for 30 min. The formazan as MTT metabolite was dissolved with 100 μL DMSO and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in the dark, followed by absorbance measurement at 570 and 650 nm, using a microplate reader spectrometer. The percentage of survival cells was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
%
S u r v i v a l =
A s
570
-
A s
650
A c
570
-
A c
650
× 100
Here, As570 and As650 are the absorbances of the samples containing carbon dots at 570 and 650 nm, and Ac570 and Ac650 are those of samples without the carbon dot [28]. Each sample was tested at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical electronic nose setup and measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the optical electronic nose system used in this work. A carbon dot aqueous solution (1 mL, 30 g L−1) was prepared, ultrasonicated for 30 min, and slowly dropped onto a 2.5 × 2.5 cm2 glass slide. The carbon dot film was then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. An optical electronic nose system was fabricated following our previous reports [25,26]. All measurements were carried out in a home-made chamber under a controlled environment and at room temperature. Nitrogen (99.9%) was used as the carrier gas, and flowed into a chamber at a rate of 500 mL min−1. A solenoid valve was used to switch the flow between the reference gas and the VOC vapors.

The FT-IR spectra of the carbon dots were obtained using an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Spectrum 2000). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was carried out using a Bruker D8 powder diffractometer. An X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos Analytical AXIS ULTRADLD) was used to characterize the surface elements of the sample. A carbon dot solution (0.02 g L−1) was dropped onto a clean aluminum substrate, which was then dried at 60 °C overnight. The optical absorbance of a carbon dot aqueous solution (0.02 g L−1) was measured using a UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-1700 PharmaSpec) and the fluorescence emission spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, FP-6200). Raman analysis was conducted using Confocal Raman spectroscopy (NT-MDT, NTEGRASpectra model) with a green light source of 532 nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images at a magnification of 100000X were captured using a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained from a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI Corporation) microscope. The hydrodynamic diameter of the carbon dots in solution (0.01 g L−1) was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a Nano Particle Analyzer (Horiba, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25 °C with scattering angle of 173°. The viscosity, pH, conductivity of solution, and refractive index were 0.89 mPa s, 12, 31.33 mS cm−1, and 1.33, respectively. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images with a magnification of 101X were captured by scanning electron microscope (Philips XL30) at an accelerating voltage of 10.2 kV.

A pristine graphene flake containing 30 carbon atoms (C30H14) was modeled and compared with our proposed carbon dot model of graphitic-nitrogen-substituted graphene with oxygen-containing functional groups at the edge (C35H16N4O9). The geometries of both models in ground state were optimized using DFT with the Minnesota functional M06-2X [29] and def2-TZVPP basis set. The effects of the bulk electrostatic component from the solvent reaction field on the relaxed geometries were also included in the optimization, using the polarizable continuum model (PCM) [30]. Time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) with the M06 density functional and PCM has been successfully used to compute excitation energies and solvatochromic shifts in liquid-phase absorption spectra [31]. In this work, the TDDFT/M06-2X/def2-TZVPP/PCM method with the ultrafine integration grid was also employed to explore electronic transitions from the ground state to the first 20 lowest excited states, with the inclusion of the solvation effect. The electronic transition energy, oscillator strength, and extinction coefficient of each transition were computed for both models in vacuo and implicit solvation. All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 program package [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and mesoporous silica
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots and mesoporous silica have both previously been synthesized from rice husk, but never simultaneously. To achieve this, the rice husk was ground and immersed in hydrochloric and sulfuric acid solutions to remove contaminants and minerals. To induce carbonization, the husk was placed in a nitric acid solution and heated using a hydrothermal method. This produced a dark brown suspension of carbon dots. We chose nitric acid over sulfuric acid because nitric acid is a weaker acid, minimizing the dissolution of silica into solution, which would contaminate the carbon dots. Furthermore, nitric acid can nitrogen-dope the carbon dots, increasing their quantum yield. The mixture was then neutralized and filtered to separate the silica precursor from the carbon dot solution. After centrifuging to remove large non-fluorescent residues, the resulting solution was purified using dialysis, and freeze-dried to yield the carbon dots. The remaining solid residue was then refluxed in a sulfuric acid solution, filtered, washed with DI water, and purified by calcination at 700 °C to yield mesoporous silica. There were no significant batch-to-batch variations and using rice husks from different sources resulted in reproducible carbon dots. The as-prepared carbon dots were then used in an alcohol vapor sensor without further surface modification (Scheme 2 ).

Although preparation of silica from rice husk has been previously reported [33], the goal in this study was to demonstrate that silica could be obtained from rice husk after carbon dot extraction. The FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared silica (Fig. 1 a) showed signals at 3440, 1114, 800, and 458 cm−1, corresponding to OH stretching, and asymmetric, symmetric, and bending vibrations of SiO2, consistent with a previous report [34]. The XRD technique was used to characterize the crystallinity of the silica. A broad peak centered at 22° showed the silica to be amorphous (Fig. 1b), consistent with a previous report [2]. The XRD and FT-IR results thus confirmed that amorphous silica had been successfully prepared in this work. The diameter of the silica was measured using DLS, and found to have an average of 100 μm (Fig. 1c). This large size was attributed to aggregation, and this was confirmed by the SEM images (Fig. 1d). Again, these results matched those previously reported for the synthesis of silica from rice husk [35]. The determination of pore size, porosity, and surface area is very important, as these factors determine the physical and chemical properties of porous silica. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analyses were used to characterize the surface area and porosity. The calculated BET surface area was 197 m2 g−1, and the BJH pore volume and pore size were 0.463 mL g−1 and 3.404 nm (Figs. S1, S2, and S3, Supplementary Information), confirming that the silica was indeed mesoporous [36]. Since mesoporous silica from rice husk has been well studied, without lengthening this paper we focused only on the analysis and applications of carbon dots for the rest of this paper.

FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS characterizations were used to identify the surface functional groups of carbon dots and their chemical composition. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) showed signals of OH at 3403 cm−1, and CC at 1583 cm−1. This confirmed the presence of hydroxyl and graphitic carbon in the structure of the dots. There were no signals at around 400-1,100 cm−1, representing silica. This indicates that carbon dots were not contaminated with silica. XPS is a surface sensitive technique used to characterize material surface. The survey XPS spectrum of carbon dots (Fig. 2b) showed signals of O1s, N1s, and C1s at 532.9 eV, 399.9 eV, and 284.9 eV. The carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen had atomic concentrations of 58.05%, 29.99%, and 1.6%. The presence of nitrogen was mainly due to the use of nitric acid in the synthesis. The high resolution of the C1s peak (Fig. 2c) confirmed the existence of graphitic and oxygenated carbon at 284.9 eV and 286.2–289.5 eV. These oxygenated carbons suggested that the thermal oxidation reactions due to hydrothermal treatment in the presence of nitric acid introduced an abundance of oxygenated groups [27]. The peaks at 73.9 eV and 118.9 eV belonged to Al2p and Al2s from the aluminum substrate used in sample preparation. The absence of silicon signals in the XPS data also confirmed that our method had completely separated the carbon dots and silica. Both the FT-IR and XPS results indicated the presence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. These functional groups have strong interactions with small molecules, making the carbon dots a good chemical probe, and are also responsible for the emission properties of the carbon dots [37,38]. The Raman spectrum exhibited a strong G band at 1594 cm-1 and a broad D band around 1200–1500 cm−1 with an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.81 (Fig. S4, Supplementary Information). The Raman result indicates the presence of sp2 carbons (G band) and sp3 defects (D band) within the carbon dots.

TEM and DLS techniques were used to characterize the size and morphology of the carbon dots. The TEM image (Fig. 2d) showed the carbon dots to be dispersed and spherical, with a diameter of about 7 nm. The HR-TEM image showed a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, indicating the presence of a graphitic core within the carbon dots. The hydrodynamic diameter determined using DLS measurement was about 4–5 nm (Fig. S5, Supplementary Information), which is consistent with the TEM results and with previous studies [39]. No large agglomerates were observed, suggesting the absence of mesoporous silica.

The carbon dots were suspended in an aqueous medium, and their optical properties were measured using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a is the UV–vis spectrum, showing broad absorption of carbon dots from 200 to 400 nm A small peak on the shoulder at 250 nm corresponded to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. A broad absorption band observed at 270–300 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bonds. At an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of the carbon dots showed a maximum emission centered at around 440 nm, corresponding to the blue emission wavelength. This is consistent with TEM results that carbon dots with size around 4–5 nm are stronger blue emitters [16,26]. It has been explained that radiative recombination between the electrons and holes, produced from the photoinduced charge separation and trapped at surface sites, contributes to the fluorescence emission of carbon dots [40–42]. The fluorescence spectra exhibited excitation-dependent properties, suggesting that the fluorescence emission originated from different emissive surface sites by different mechanisms [43]. The inset in Fig. 3a shows that the carbon dot solution was blue under UV illumination at 254 nm (inset). The quantum yield of the carbon dots in aqueous solution at pH7 was determined to be 3%, which was not previously reported in other carbon dot/silica systems from rice husk.

The physical/chemical stability of carbon dots is important for practical applications, and therefore, the photostability of carbon dots in aqueous solution was investigated in two conditions: under ambient conditions for four weeks, and under UV illumination at 365 nm using a 30 W lamp for 6 h. No change in emission intensity was observed in either case. (Fig. 3b and c). There was no obvious photobleaching or change in colour observed. Under UV irradiation of 6 h, the fluorescence emission and optical absorption of the carbon dots remained above 80%. These results confirmed that the rice husk-derived carbon dots are excellent fluorophores for applications that require excellent photostability.

Since biocompatibility is of great importance for carbon dots to be employed in biomedical applications, they were then subjected to the cytotoxicity testing using MTT assay on MCF-7 breast cancer cells. A carbon dot concentration of 80 μg mL−1 was used, with an exposure time to cells of 72 h (Fig. 3d). The carbon dots demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, with a cell viability greater than 80% after incubation. This result suggests that the carbon dots from rice husk are potential biocompatible nanomaterials for use in various bio-related applications such as nanomedicine, cell imaging, and biosensing.

The stable optical properties and the presence of surface functional groups of carbon dots allow them to be used as sensing nanomaterials for the detection of metal ions, chemical compounds, and biomolecules [44]. In addition, carbon dots are promising nanomaterials for the detection of VOCs, especially polar ones, due to their large surface area and the hydrophilic functional groups as binding sites. However, the use of carbon dots as sensors for VOC detection and determination is rare [24]. In this work, a carbon dot film was combined with an optical electronic nose system and used to sense alcohol vapor. An electronic nose mimics the olfactory system, and such systems are widely used in environmental monitoring, food and beverage testing, and clinical and other sensing applications [45,46]. An electronic nose system typically consists of a gas controller, a transducer, and a data acquisition/processing unit. Several types of electronic nose have been developed, based on electrical conductivity, optical properties, and mass transductions [47]. In this study, the optical technique was used because of the excellent photostability of the carbon dots and ease of sample preparation and instrument setup.

Methanol and ethanol vapors were used as the analytes. The sensor responses (Fig. 4 a) corresponding to the variation in the light intensity transmitted through the carbon dot film were measured. When the methanol or ethanol vapor was flowed to the thin film, the sensor response greatly and rapidly reduced. On the contrary, when the nitrogen reference gas was flowed, the sensor response increased. This result indicated that the carbon dots could detect the methanol and ethanol vapors. When the nitrogen gas and alcohol vapor were repeatedly flowing to the carbon dot film, the modulation of sensor response over several cycles was observed. This confirmed that the carbon dots exhibited great stability toward alcohol vapors and no deterioration on the film was observed. Following exposure to ethanol, the film regained its original intensity after being puffed with nitrogen gas for 80.0 ± 9.1 s. Following exposure to methanol, recovery took 82.0 ± 4.2 s. This suggested that methanol took slightly longer than ethanol to desorb from the surface of the carbon dots. This may be due to the smaller size and higher polarity of methanol than ethanol, which increased adsorption by and interaction with the carbon dot film. The sensitivity value of the carbon dots was further quantified using Eq. (2).
(2)
S e n s i t i v i t y
=
I
A l c o h o l
-
I R
I R
where IAlcohol and IR are the sensor responses of the alcohol vapor and reference gas, respectively. The sensitivity value of carbon dot-detected ethanol was 0.025 and that of methanol was 0.024 (Tables S1 and S2, Supplementary information). Hence, the selectivity of the carbon dots toward methanol and ethanol could not be distinguished simply by calculating the response time or sensitivity value.

To distinguish between methanol and ethanol vapors, principal component analysis (PCA) was then applied to analyze patterns of high-dimensional data, and was used to classify and differentiate methanol, ethanol, and their mixtures at different concentrations and provided a graphic output [48]. Five repeated cycles of dynamic responses of the eight LEDs were used as the input data. The score plots were obtained, in which the circles mark exclusive zones of the data points. The discrimination of alcohols was excellent based on the first two principal components (PC1 and PC2), which accounted for as much as 99.5% for the total variance. Furthermore, no overlap between clusters for methanol and ethanol was observed (Fig. 4b). The results indicated that the carbon dots could effectively differentiate between the two alcohols and determine their ratios in the mixture.

The optical absorbance of the carbon dot film was further investigated using UV–vis spectroscopy. A quartz slide was used for the carbon dot film preparation and its absorbance in the absence and presence of methanol and ethanol vapor was measured. Absorbance of carbon dot film increased when the methanol and ethanol vapors were flowed to the film (Fig. 4c). This suggested that the optical absorptivity of the film became enhanced, reducing transmitted light when exposed to alcohol vapor. This reduced transmitted light was consistent with the electronic nose results as described above. These unique optical properties of carbon dots allow them to detect the alcohol vapors, and to discriminate between alcohol types using optical electronic nose and PCA. The reason that an increase in absorbance was smaller than the decrease in sensor response in electronic nose is due to the thinner film used to prevent the absorbance from becoming saturated.

The ability of the carbon dots to characterize a range of concentrations of alcohols when used in an optical electronic nose was further tested. Ethanol (15, 30, and 45% v/v) and methanol (5, 25, 50, and 75% v/v) solutions in water were used as vapor sources. The sensor responses decreased when the film was exposed to the alcohol vapors at all tested concentrations (Fig. 5 a and b). The PCA score plots were obtained for different ethanol and methanol concentrations (Fig. 5c and d). It was found that PCA score plots of both methanol and ethanol were well separated, especially at high concentrations. We attributed this observation to a larger change in optical properties when high concentrations of alcohols were used. A real spirit sample (35% alcohol content) was next used as the vapor source. When the vapor from spirit sample was flowed to the film, the sensor response dropped rapidly. The PCA score plot of the spirit sample overlapped well with those of the 30% and 45% standard ethanol solutions, consistent with the alcohol content specified on the bottle label. This confirmed the ability of the carbon dots to quantify the alcohol contents of a real alcoholic beverage. The large data scattering or circle in the score plot produced by the spirit sample was attributed to the presence of matrices or interferences, as the sample was used as received, and not purified further. The carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose has shown promising performance and can be used for real-time vapor sensing for environmental research, and in the food and beverage sector.

We next investigated the ability of carbon dots to detect other solvent vapors, including acetone, diethyl ether, toluene, dichloromethane, and hexane, as these VOCs are commonly used in laboratories and industry. Exposure to VOCs over a long period of time can cause serious health problems. Detection of VOCs at ambient environments using practical devices is therefore of great practical interest. The sensor responses showed that carbon dots were sensitive to these vapors (Fig. 6 a). Difference in sensor response magnitudes indicates the difference in selectivity and sensitivity of carbon dots toward VOCs. Based on PCA analysis (Fig. 6b), we found that our carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose was able to differentiate between methanol, ethanol, acetone, and diethyl ether. Hexane, toluene, and dichloromethane are possibly not polar enough to be distinguished by the carbon dot-integrated electronic nose, resulting in overlapping PCA score plots. The PCA plot also showed a polarity trend for the tested solvents, which agrees with the polarity scale,
E T N
[49]. This result also confirms that the selective detection of solvent vapor by carbon dots is mainly the result of polar-polar interaction between the solvent molecules and the carbon dots. This is similar to previous study in which the optical properties of carbon dots strongly depends on the polarity of the solvent [50]. Since our electronic nose is sensitive to various VOCs, it suggests applications in environmental, health, and safety monitoring, and in quality control.

The effects of solvents on the optical absorbance of carbon dots were further evaluated by dissolution of carbon dots in organic solvents with various polarities instead of a thin film fabrication (Fig. 6c). As the carbon dot thin film yielded a small change in absorbance when exposed to solvents as shown in Fig. 4c, the differentiation of the effects of solvent using a thin film is a difficult task. The measured absorbance of carbon dots in organic solvents was in the following order: water > methanol > diethyl ether > dichloromethane > hexane. This suggests that the absorptivity of carbon dots increases as the polarity of the medium increases because carbon dots are hydrophilic and strongly interact with polar solvents. The result is in a good agreement with the polarity trend as shown by the optical electronic nose, indicating that the polar-polar interaction is possibly the main effect resulting in a change in the polarization and optical properties of carbon dots, which were detected by the optical electronic nose.

To investigate the effects of solvent polarization on the carbon dots, the electronic absorption spectra were simulated using TDDFT and PCM. Prior to TDDFT calculations of the vertical electronic excitations, two graphene-based structures were modeled and optimized as shown in Fig. 7 a and b. As confirmed by the XPS and FT-IR spectra, carbon dots contain both a graphene domain and hydrophilic functional groups. Unlike the model of sp2 carbon arranged in a graphene-like sheet (Gr), the model structure for carbon dots is a nitrogen-doped graphene functionalized with hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Electronic excitation energies and oscillator strengths for the spin-singlet excited states of the two models were computed in vacuo as well as in implicit solvents (Tables S3 and S4, Supplementary Information). Regardless of the environment of solvation, electronic transitions were computationally found in both the UV and visible spectral regions. However, the strongest transition for Gr occurred at 283 nm in gas phase, and slightly shifted to 290 nm in methanol and 291 nm in ethanol (Fig. 7c and Table S3, Supplementary Information). In comparison, the carboxyl group is covered by electron donors so that its strongest transition showed at 280–282 nm (Fig. 7d and Table S4, Supplementary Information). These computational results were in good agreement with the experimental UV–vis spectrum of carbon dots in water, showing shoulder peaks in the UV spectral region below 300 nm (Fig. 3a). This gives us confidence that our DFT models sufficiently describe the electronic nature of carbon dots. Additionally, the computed extinction coefficients for both models increased, compared with those in gas phase, upon solvation in methanol and ethanol (Fig. 7c and d). This correlates to (i) the increase in light transmission due to the adsorption of alcohol vapors on the sensing platform in the electronic nose and (ii) the enhanced optical UV absorption of carbon dots by methanol. It has also been reported that the large dipole moment of chromophores noncovalently bound to graphene produces a larger effect on the energy gap of a hybrid system, producing higher efficiency Raman scattering [51]. The change in optical absorption is believed to arise from the change of polarity/dipole moment around the graphene sheets when exposed to solvent vapors [52]. This highlights the importance of the interfacial electronic coupling between the graphene sheets and alcohol vapors.

In our experimental studies, the optical absorption of carbon dots was sensitive to adsorption of solvent vapor molecules, as confirmed by the electronic nose and UV–vis results (Fig. 8 ). The solvation effect of VOC on the carbon dots led to a change in optical absorptivity. Polar-polar interactions of various degrees resulted in the different sensitivities of carbon dots toward different VOCs. The DFT calculations further supported the experimental results, as the extinction coefficients of carbon dots were enhanced when solvated by alcohols. The modulation of optical absorption of carbon-based nanomaterials upon adsorption of solvent molecules has been previously calculated and reported. Zhao et al. also used TDDFT and PCM to calculate the absorption spectra of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in the bulk solvents, i.e. toluene and dichloromethane. They found that the oscillator strength of GQDs in toluene and dichloromethane increased compared with those in gas phase, which is consistent with our experimental and computational results [53]. The work reported here is however the first to demonstrate the modulation of optical absorption of carbon dots both experimentally and computationally. Based on our work and previous reports, carbon dots have been demonstrated to be sensitive nanomaterials for the detection of solvents, with potential applications in a range of fields. The sensitivity of our current optical electronic nose is approximately one order of magnitude lower than those of previous reported alcohol vapor sensor, such as graphene-based materials [54], metal-based materials [55], carbon nanotubes [56], and conductive polymers [57]. However, our carbon dots are much simpler to synthesize and more optically stable and our sensor can be operated at room temperature, opening a much wider range of potential practical applications. The previously reported sensors need to be operated at high temperature, or require complex material synthesis and sensor fabrication. The carbon dot-integrated optical electronic nose developed in this work offers an alternative choice as a simple, room-temperature alcohol vapor and VOC sensor.

In this work, we synthesized carbon dots and mesoporous silica from rice husk. The carbon dots had diameters of 4–5 nm and a quantum yield of 3%, and exhibited blue emission with excellent photostability. We were able to integrate carbon dots into an optical electronic nose and use these to detect and identify methanol and ethanol and to quantify the alcohol content of a commercial beverage. Furthermore, our system can be used to sense and distinguish VOCs based on polar-polar interfacial interaction. When exposed to solvent molecules, the modulation of optical properties of carbon dots were modulated as confirmed both experimentally and computationally. The DFT calculations showed an increase in optical absorbance when carbon dots interacted with solvent molecules, consistent with the UV–vis and electronic nose results. These carbon dots are thus suitable for real-time sensing of alcohols and VOCs. The unique physicochemical properties in reversible binding and highly interactive polarity of carbon dots open a range of sensing applications and further utilization as optically stable, sensitive probes. Carbon dots are thus potential non-toxic fluorescent materials with a range of applications, including bioimaging and sensing while mesoporous silica is an essential ingredient in composite materials. Based on these results, rice husk can be turned from an agricultural waste to a precursor of two high-value products.

We would like to acknowledge the use of computing resources from (1) the Science Cloud Project at Department of Computer Science, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University; (2) the Energy Promotion and Conservation Fund and National Science and Technology Development Agency (P-17-50509); and (3) National e-Science Infrastructure Consortium.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2018.09.077.
